sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 144, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 143 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 4.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
SPACE didn't complete the level. Both feet are at the bottom (rows 54-58), but at different columns (14-18 and 39-43).
Let me think about what the goal might be. Maybe the goal is to get the feet to the same column, or to specific positions.
Looking at the body shape, the left feet are at cols 14-18 (left side of the body), and the right feet are at cols 39-43 (right side of the body). The body extends from cols 9-53.
Maybe the goal is to get the feet to the same row AND column, i.e., both feet at the same position. But that doesn't make sense for a "person" shape.
Alternatively, maybe the goal is to get the feet to specific "target" positions on the body. Let me look at the body shape more carefully.
Actually, let me reconsider the problem. The body is a black shape, and the feet are blue squares. The feet move within the body. Maybe the goal is to get the feet to positions where they match some pattern or symmetry.
Let me look at the initial state again:
- Left feet: rows 19-23, cols 19-23
- Right feet: rows 54-58, cols 44-48
The left feet are at the left arm, and the right feet are at the right arm. This looks like a "reaching" position.
Current state:
- Left feet: rows 54-58, cols 14-18
- Right feet: rows 54-58, cols 39-43
Both feet are at the bottom. This looks like a "standing" position.
Maybe the goal is to get the feet to the head (rows 9-13, cols 24-48). Let me try to get the feet to the head.
To get the left feet to the head:
- Rows: from 54-58 to 9-13 (need to move up by 45 rows, 9 UP actions)
- Cols: from 14-18 to 24-48 (need to move right by 10-34 cols, 2-7 RIGHT actions)
To get the right feet to the head:
- Rows: from 54-58 to 9-13 (need to move up by 45 rows, 9 UP actions)
- Cols: from 39-43 to 24-48 (need to move left by 0-19 cols, 0-4 LEFT actions)
But the feet move in opposite horizontal directions. So if I press RIGHT, the left feet move right and the right feet move left. If I press LEFT, the left feet move left and the right feet move right.
This means I can't independently control the horizontal positions of the feet. The horizontal distance between the feet changes with LEFT/RIGHT actions.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to get the feet to specific positions that require a specific sequence of moves.
Actually, let me try a different approach. Let me look at the body shape and see if there are any "holes" or "targets" that the feet need to reach.
Looking at the segmentation, the body is a single black node with rows 9-58, cols 9-53. The feet are blue nodes within the body.
Maybe the goal is to get the feet to positions where they are symmetric with respect to the body's center.
The body's center is at row 33.5, col 31. The left feet are at rows 54-58, cols 14-18, and the right feet are at rows 54-58, cols 39-43.
The left feet are 17-21 cols left of the center, and the right feet are 8-12 cols right of the center. This is not symmetric.
Let me try to make the feet symmetric. To do this, I need to move the left feet right and the right feet left (press RIGHT) until they are equidistant from the center.
Actually, let me first check the horizontal range of the feet. The left feet are at cols 14-18, and the right feet are at cols 39-43. The distance between them is 21 cols (39 - 18 = 21).
If I press RIGHT, the left feet move right by 5 cols and the right feet move left by 5 cols. So the distance between them decreases by 10 cols.
If I press LEFT, the left feet move left by 5 cols and the right feet move right by 5 cols. So the distance between them increases by 10 cols.
Let me try pressing RIGHT to see if the feet can move closer together.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current feet: rows 54-58, cols 14-18 (left) and rows 54-58, cols 39-43 (right)
# Let me try pressing RIGHT to move feet closer together horizontally

action(['RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        print(f"Feet: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Try pressing RIGHT again
action(['RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        print(f"Feet after 2nd RIGHT: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Feet: rows 54-58, cols 19-23
Feet: rows 54-58, cols 34-38
Feet after 2nd RIGHT: rows 54-58, cols 24-28
Feet after 2nd RIGHT: rows 54-58, cols 34-38
